coredata.dc:title: Integrated ratiometric fluorescence probe-based acoustofluidic platform for visual detection of anthrax biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiafeng
Chen, Panpan
Chen, Jie
Ye, Xiangxue
Cao, Shurui
Sun, Chuqiang
Jin, Yang
Zhang, Liying
Du, Shuhu
coredata.dc:description: The sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an excellent biomarker of Bacillus anthracis, especially through visual point-of-care testing, is significant for accurate and rapid diagnosis of anthrax to timely prevent anthrax disease or biological terrorist attack. Herein, an acoustofluidics-based colorimetric platform with the integrated ratiometric fluorescence probe (INT-probe) was fabricated, which improved the sensitivity of visual detection for DPA and overcame the poor reproducibility of the existing acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric analysis. For the design of INT-probe, Eu3+-EDTA complex as sensing moiety was grafted onto the surface of blue organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs)-doped SiO2 nanoparticles (NPs). Upon exposure to DPA, Eu3+ was sensitized by DPA to emit red luminescence, while the SiCDs as reference inside the SiO2 NPs still kept the blue fluorescence unchanged. Attributed to the acoustic radiation force-driven enrichment of the INT-probe, slight color changes caused by low concentration of DPA could be amplified and distinguished by naked-eyes/smartphone. With the increase of DPA concentration, obvious color variations of INT-probe/DPA aggregates from blue to pink could be observed, and the color information of the fluorescent aggregates was converted to red, green and blue values for quantitative analysis, whose lowest detectable concentration reached 100 nM that is about 2–3 orders of magnitude lower than the infectious dosage of Bacillus anthracis spores (60 μM). Importantly, benefiting from the great color signal enhancement by acoustofluidic sensing platform, the usage of Eu3+ reduced to as low as 0.273 μmol per gram of SiO2 NPs, providing a meaningful way to utilize lanthanide resource efficiently.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acoustofluidics
Integrated ratiometric fluorescence probe
Anthrax biomarker
Visual detection
Dipicolinic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of SiCDs-doped SiO2 NPs with amino-functionalization
Measurement of DPA by acoustofluidic platform
Colorimetric assay of DPA via acoustofluidic platform
Selectivity, anti-interference and practicability of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the design strategy for the INT-probe. (B–D) TEM images of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs, SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs and INT-probe, respectively. (E) Survey XPS spectrum of the INT-probe. (F–J) Fitted XPS spectra of the Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O1s and Eu 3d regions for the INT-probe. (K) HAADF-STEM and element mapping of (L) Si, (M) O, (N) C, (O) N and (P) Eu signal in the INT-probe.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation (λem = 460 nm) and emission (λex = 270 nm) spectra of SiCDs. The insets are photographs of the SiCDs colloid taken under daylight (left, a) and UV LED with λ = 254 nm (right, b). (B) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 270 nm) of SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs, Eu3+, DPA and the INT-probe in the absence and presence of DPA, respectively.

Fluorescence aggregate images taken by smartphone (A) after and (B) before acoustofluidic concentration of the mixtures of the INT-probe and DPA with different concentration ranging from 0 to 7.00 μM. (C) Plot of R/B ratio versus cDPA (μM).

(A) R/B value responses to various substances (front) and their mixture with DPA (back). Front bars: the selectivity experiments were done in Tris-HCl buffer solution (5 mM, pH 7.4) with the addition of DPA or other various substances into INT-probe solution. (1) blank, (2) DPA, (3) BA, (4) o-PA, (5) m-PA, (6) p-PA, (7) Glu, (8) Gly, (9) Phe, (10) Ca2+, (11) Mg2+, (12) Na+, (13) K+, (14) Cl−, (15) NO3 −, (16) HCO3 −, (17) BHb, (18) BSA, (19) glucose and (20) FBS. Back bars: the anti-interferences capabilities were tested using the mixture of an excessive substance with DPA. The concentration of DPA is 3 μM. The concentrations of K+, Na+, NO3−, HCO3−, Cl−, Phe, Glu, Gly and BA are all 30 μM, those of p-PA, o-PA, m-PA, Mg2+, Ca2+, BHb, BSA and Glucose are 3 μM. FBS sample was diluted 10 times. (B and C) The corresponding fluorescence aggregate images captured by the smartphone under a 254 nm UV LED.
Schematic diagram of the BAWs-based device (left) and work mechanism of the acoustofluidics-assisted visual testing for DPA (right).
Schematic drawing for the visual detection of DPA using acoustofluidic platform.

head.ce:title: Integrated ratiometric fluorescence probe-based acoustofluidic platform for visual detection of anthrax biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors Jiafeng Wu and Panpan Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an excellent biomarker of Bacillus anthracis, especially through visual point-of-care testing, is significant for accurate and rapid diagnosis of anthrax to timely prevent anthrax disease or biological terrorist attack. Herein, an acoustofluidics-based colorimetric platform with the integrated ratiometric fluorescence probe (INT-probe) was fabricated, which improved the sensitivity of visual detection for DPA and overcame the poor reproducibility of the existing acoustofluidics-assisted colorimetric analysis. For the design of INT-probe, Eu3+-EDTA complex as sensing moiety was grafted onto the surface of blue organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs)-doped SiO2 nanoparticles (NPs). Upon exposure to DPA, Eu3+ was sensitized by DPA to emit red luminescence, while the SiCDs as reference inside the SiO2 NPs still kept the blue fluorescence unchanged. Attributed to the acoustic radiation force-driven enrichment of the INT-probe, slight color changes caused by low concentration of DPA could be amplified and distinguished by naked-eyes/smartphone. With the increase of DPA concentration, obvious color variations of INT-probe/DPA aggregates from blue to pink could be observed, and the color information of the fluorescent aggregates was converted to red, green and blue values for quantitative analysis, whose lowest detectable concentration reached 100 nM that is about 2–3 orders of magnitude lower than the infectious dosage of Bacillus anthracis spores (60 μM). Importantly, benefiting from the great color signal enhancement by acoustofluidic sensing platform, the usage of Eu3+ reduced to as low as 0.273 μmol per gram of SiO2 NPs, providing a meaningful way to utilize lanthanide resource efficiently.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis), the spores of which can invade human and animal through skin wound and respiratory or digestive tract, is the causative organism of anthrax (Dixon et al., 1999; Hanna, 1998; Koehler, 2002; Mock and Fouet, 2001; Turnbull, 2002). Inhalation of over 104 B. anthracis spores will be lethal with a mortality rate of almost 100% unless receiving timely treatment within 24–48 h (Hurtle et al., 2004; King et al., 2003). Moreover, B. anthracis spore is considered as a fatal biological warfare agent and can be abused due to its high resistance to environmental stress and simplified production process outside the laboratory (Barakat et al., 2002; Borio et al., 2001; Inglesby et al., 2002). Therefore, rapid and sensitive detection of B. anthracis spores is extremely important to avoid anthrax infection and prevent potential bioterrorism. It is worth noting that 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (also known as dipicolinic acid, DPA) accounts for 5–15% of B. anthracis spore's dry mass and cannot be found in other common spores. Thus, DPA is considered as an excellent biomarker of the bacillus spores (Cable et al., 2007; Setlow et al., 2016). In the past decades, various techniques have been developed to detect DPA, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Bai et al., 2017; Cheung et al., 2016), mass spectrometry (Voorhees et al., 2000), electrochemical assay (Zhou et al., 2005), colorimetry (Cheng et al., 2019; Yin and Tong, 2021), ratiometric fluorescence method (Luan et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019; Na et al., 2020; Qu et al., 2019; Xing et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019), and so on. Among them, ratiometric fluorescence method with self-calibration capability is less susceptible to analyte-independent factors (such as background fluorescence, instrumental fluctuation and operational parameters) than that of single-emission fluorescence, and improves precision and sensitivity of the analytical method (Yang et al., 2019). Moreover, based on the change of fluorescence intensity ratio, ratiometric fluorescence probes can achieve profuse color evolution rather than only brightness change, realizing the visual detection of target analytes (Li et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020), and freeing detection from bulky equipment and professionals.

So far, most of ratiometric fluorescence probes for DPA detection are based on lanthanide ion-sensitized luminesce and several of them have realized visual detection of DPA. However, due to the fact that slight fluorescence color changes are hard to distinguish by naked eyes, the sensitivity of visual detection still needs to be improved. Meanwhile, excessive amount of lanthanide resource is consumed during probe synthesis, resulting in an inefficient utilization of lanthanide resource, time-consuming and laborious postprocess (washing or dialysis). In addition, for test strip-based sensors, they often suffer from an inhomogeneous distribution of fluorescent substances after interaction between probe and analyte solution, since probes are not firmly attached to test strip. The accuracy of quantitation, especially at low DPA concentration, is not satisfied. To overcome the above shortcomings, a new technology needs to be integrated with fluorescence colorimetric assays to improve sensitivity and reliability of detection, and to utilize the lanthanide resource efficiently.

Acoustofluidics, which integrates acoustic manipulation with microfluidic devices, has revealed enormous capability in bioanalytical chemistry (Li and Huang, 2019; Ozcelik et al., 2018; Rufo et al., 2022) by virtue of advantages like simplicity in fabrication, contact-free manipulation, low cost, low power consumption and capability of miniaturization/integration (Ding et al., 2013). So far, numerous acoustofluidic techniques have been demonstrated to perform bioanalytical chemistry-oriented functions such as sample concentration (Collins et al., 2016; Destgeer et al., 2016; Jakobsson et al., 2015), fluorescence-activated cell sorter (Kalb et al., 2017), cell/particle separation (Chen et al., 2016), fluid/droplet manipulation (Ashtiani et al., 2018) and many more. In the previous work, we have demonstrated that acoustofluidic method can be used to enrich red and green fluorescence nanoparticles (SiO2@R NPs as a signal report unit and G@SiO2 NPs as an internal reference) to improve the sensitivity of fluorescence colorimetric assays (Zhang et al., 2020). With the activation of acoustic signal, SiO2@R NPs and G@SiO2 NPs were enriched by acoustic radiation force producing from the scattering of the acoustic waves, and the localized particle concentration was increased. Accordingly, the fluorescence changes caused by the interaction between target analyte and SiO2@R NPs were amplified especially in the low concentration of analyte. As a result, the sensitivity of fluorescence colorimetry was improved with low sample consumption and rapid readout. Notably, despite the sensitivity has been improved, the repeatability of the method needs to be further enhanced due to color inhomogeneity of formed fluorescent aggregates. It may be caused by simply mixing two kinds of fluorescence probes NPs, which cannot be completely identical in physicochemical properties (e.g., size and surface potential). So, it is essential to develop a more robust ratiometric probe system in which internal reference and sensing moiety are present in the same carrier.

Herein, to reduce the effect of color inhomogeneity on readout and improve the reliability of visual detection, Eu3+-EDTA complex as a signal report unit was grafted onto the surface of organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs)-doped SiO2 NPs, resulting in an integrated ratiometric fluorescence probe (INT-probe) for sensitive and visual detection of DPA via smartphone-based acoustofluidic sensing platform. As for detection, europium ions from the INT-probe were sensitized by DPA to exhibit red fluorescence “off-on” response, while the blue fluorescence of entrapped SiCDs as an internal reference still maintain unchanged. To improve the detection sensitivity, after the interaction between INT-probe and DPA, the bulk acoustic waves (BAWs)-based acoustofluidic sensing platform was utilized to concentrate the INT-probe NPs. The resulting NPs aggregates exhibited strong and homogenous fluorescence, which could be distinguished by naked eyes. Then the formed fluorescent aggregate was further photographed using a smartphone camera and the concentration of DPA was quantitatively measured by analyzing the red, green and blue (RGB) values of these images. Considering the increasing threats to rare-earth elements supplies and price rise due to growing demands (Wang et al., 2017), the amount of Eu3+ grafted to the INT-probe was specially investigated to utilize lanthanide resource efficiently and avoid the postprocess of metal chelation from time-consuming washing or dialysis steps. Benefiting from the great color signal enhancement by acoustofluidic sensing platform, the Eu3+ amount of as low as 0.273 μmol per gram of SiO2 NPs was adequate for the INT-probe to response positively to DPA with the lowest detectable concentration of 100 nM. Meanwhile, an obvious color change ranging from blue to pink was exhibited. In addition, to verify the feasibility of practical application, fetal calf serum was chosen and spiked with DPA for its detection. It has been demonstrated that the combination of acoustofluidic NPs enrichment with ratiometric fluorescence colorimetry enables high sensitivity for visual detection. Compared with the fluorescence change of the analyte-probe complexes in dispersed state, that of the proposed method can be observed even when DPA concentration is low. More importantly, the use of the INT-probe has overcome the limitations of previous acoustofluidics-based fluorescence colorimetric assays and provides more reliable fluorescence signal for quantification. With the specific advantages of low cost, small sample volume (150 μL) and short detection time (less than 5 min), we believe that acoustofluidics-assisted visual platform with the integrated probe will be a prospective method for DPA detection and many other point-of-care testing (POCT) applications.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), concentrated ammonia aqueous solution (25 wt %), anhydrous ethanol, citric acid (CA), petroleum ether, calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), benzoic acid (BA), p-phthalic acid (p-PA), m-phthalic acid (m-PA) and o-phthalic acid (o-PA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS), ethylenediaminetetraacetic dianhydride (EDTAD), europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), phenylalanine (Phe), bovine hemoglobin (BHb) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pyridine (99.5%, with molecular sieves, Water ≤50 ppm), dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.8%, with molecular sieves, Water ≤50 ppm) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). EDTA mono anhydride (denoted as EDTMD) was prepared according to the reported method (Rieter et al., 2007) with some modifications, and the details of synthesis and related characterization were provided in Supplementary Material (Fig. S1). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in this work.

UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Luminescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp source and a long-pass filter was used to eliminate the interference from the second-order scattering peak of the excitation wavelength. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Tensor-27 spectrometer in the transmittance mode at a resolution of 4 cm−1 (Bruker, German). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) characterization was performed on a JEOL JEM-2010 instrument (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements and zeta potential measurements of NPs were performed on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 instrument (Malvern, UK). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping was carried out by an Oxford X-max instrument (Oxford, UK) to investigate the dispersion of the element in NPs. A function generator (AFG3011G, Tektronix, USA) was utilized to actuate the acoustofluidic system.

SiCDs were synthesized by a modified hydrothermal route based on a previous literature (Wang et al., 2011). Briefly, CA (0.5 g), AEAPMS (10 mL) and deionized water (10 mL) were sealed into a Teflon equipped stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C in a drying oven for 12 h. The final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times, and then the as-prepared SiCDs were dispersed in ethanol with a volume ratio of 1:1 and kept in a refrigerating chamber (4 °C) prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SiCDs-doped SiO2 NPs with amino-functionalization
ce:section.ce:para: SiCDs-doped SiO2 NPs with amino-functionalization, denoted as SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs, were synthesized based on the Stöber method. First, colloid A (10 μL of SiCDs, 1.5 mL of TEOS, 400 μL of APTES and 9 mL of ethanol) and solution B (3.4 mL of NH3·H2O and 15 mL of ethanol) were separately prepared. Then, colloid A was added (via a micro feed pump at a rate of 150 μL/min) into a round-flask that contained solution B under vigorous stirring at room temperature. The resulting mixture was allowed to react for 12 h. After the reaction, the products were collected by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 2 min) and washed several times with ethanol. The as-prepared SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs were redispersed in ethanol, and then dried in vacuum oven for further use.

The INT-probe (SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA-Eu3+ NPs) was subsequently synthesized by modifying SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs with EDTA and Eu3+. First, SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs powder (300 mg) was dispersed in a mixture of anhydrous DMF (30 mL) and pyridine (10 mL) by sonication. Subsequently, EDTMD (150 mg) was added into above mixture and sonicated for 5 min. The resulting system was stirred under a nitrogen flow and heated at 40 °C for 16 h to obtain SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs. The product was separated by centrifuging and washed several times with hexane, and then dried in oven for further characterizations and experiments. To chelate Eu3+ onto the surface of SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs, 200 mg of SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and sodium hydroxide was added to adjust pH to 7.4, and then 10 μL of EuCl3 aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) was added into the above NPs colloid and the mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA-Eu3+ NPs were collected by centrifugation and redispersed in deionized water to reach a concentration of 25 mg/mL for further use.

The acoustofluidic platform consists of a BAWs-based device, a 254 nm ultraviolet light-emitting diode (UV LED) equipped with a bandpass filter, a smartphone (iphone 12, Apple, USA) (with a 200 × custom-designed magnification lens) and a compact 3D printed chamber. Autodesk computer aided design (AutoCAD) program was utilized to design the 3D model which was then printed by a 3D printer. The diagram of the sensing platform and the inner structure is displayed in Fig. S2. The BAWs-based device was fabricated according to the previous report (Zhang et al., 2020). Briefly, a square glass capillary (with the inner dimension d = 0.2 mm and the wall thickness of 0.10 mm) was bonded onto the surface of a piezoelectric transducer (PZT) (20 mm × 15 mm × 2.1 mm) using UV light adhesive, and the obtained chip was mounted onto a glass slide (7.5 cm × 2.5 cm × 1 mm) with two small hard plastic sheets, which separated the PZT and the glass slide to reduce acoustic wave energy loss from the piezoelectric thin film. In addition, both ends of the capillary were connected to 0.5 mm inner diameter polyethylene tubes, providing the inlet and outlet for liquids. The photograph of the device was shown in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of DPA by acoustofluidic platform
ce:section.ce:para: Different amounts of DPA were added to 150 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (5 mM, pH 7.4) containing the INT-probes to reach a final volume of 300 μL. All the colorimetric experiments were conducted using the acoustofluidic platform. First, the suspension containing the analyte and the INT-probe (1.25 mg/mL) was injected into the acoustofluidic device from the inlet using a syringe. After the suspension within the capillary remained stable, a function generator was utilized to drive the PZT with a frequency of 3.737 MHz. Under the action of acoustic driving force, the INT-probe NPs combined with DPA were concentrated to form aggregates in the capillary, and the fluorescence images of aggregates were taken by smartphone for further quantitation of the DPA levels using ImageJ software.

Generally, fluorescence intensity of probe is proportional to its concentration in the medium. For visual detection, high concentration of probe is essentially necessary due to the insensitivity of naked eyes to weak color signal. However, for the single-responsive probe, especially the “off-on” mode, a high concentration could make little contribution to improve visual sensitivity unless analyte-responded probe possesses a high quantum yield. Therefore, to improve the sensitivity of visual detection, it is advisable to further aggregate the fluorescence probes. Herein, combined with the standing BAWs-based device, acoustofluidics-assisted enrichment of fluorescent probes can enhance visual sensitivity of colorimetric assay using as few probes as possible.

Scheme 1 shows a schematic of the BAWs-based device and the work mechanism for the acoustofluidics-assisted fluorescence colorimetry. The device is fabricated by bonding a square glass capillary onto the surface of PZT using epoxy glue. Details of the fabrication are described in the experiment section. Based on the piezoelectric effect, the electrical energy will be converted to mechanical energy once an electrical excitation signal is applied to PZT through a function generator. With an activation frequency, the z direction resonating mode of PZT will be excited, and the generated BAWs will propagate along the thickness-direction into the glass capillary. By matching the half wavelength of the acoustic wave to the inner width of the capillary, the resonance of the BAWs inside the capillary results in a standing BAW field with a pressure node at the channel center, and the generated acoustic driving forces can be used to manipulate particles inside capillary. Driven by the force, suspended particles will migrate either toward the pressure nodes or antinodes, depending on the properties of the particle and fluid media (Fornell et al., 2015; Yiannacou and Sariola, 2021; Zhang et al., 2014). When particles possess higher compressibility and lower densities relative to the surrounding media, they will focus along the pressure antinode, whereas particles with the opposite properties will focus along the pressure nodes (Johnson et al., 2013; Shields IV et al., 2014). In this work, SiO2 NPs was chosen as a sensing carrier with the advantages of the high surface-to-volume ratio and ease to be modified. When acoustics is on, the SiO2-based INT-probe particles will be driven to the pressure nodes due to the relatively lower compressibility and higher density of SiO2 particles than the aqueous solution. A strong color signal can be observed (the right-most photographs of Scheme 1) compared with the state of acoustics OFF, The acoustic wave frequency is calculated according to the following equation (Barnkob and Bruus, 2009; Yiannacou and Sariola, 2021):
f
x , y
=
c 2
(
n x
l x
)
2
+
(
n y
l y
)
2
where c is the acoustic velocity in medium, l x and l y are the length and width of the capillary, respectively, and n x and n y are the mode numbers. Here, to generate a pressure node line along the center of the glass capillary, the equation can be simplified to f 0,1 = c/2d, where c is the acoustic velocity (∼1495 m/s) in water (Hao et al., 2020) and d is the inner dimension of the capillary. A square glass capillary with its inner diameter of 0.2 mm is chosen in this study, which gives f 0,1 = 3.737 MHz as the first resonance in the width direction. At this frequency, the animation of the motion of SiO2 NPs is given in Movie S1. It can be observed that all SiO2 NPs are enriched along the center of the glass capillary within 2 min.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2022.114538.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

The INT-probe was prepared by doping SiCDs (as internal reference) into SiO2 NPs and further grafting Eu3+-EDTA complex (as sensing moiety) onto the surface of SiO2 NPs (Fig. 1 A). In brief, the blue emitting-SiCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method, using CA as the carbon source and AEAPMS as a passivating agent. Then, SiCDs-doped SiO2 NPs with amino functionalization were prepared through Stöber method, in which SiCDs, TEOS and APTES hydrolyzed and crosslinked into monodisperse SiO2 NPs under catalysis of ammonia. Next, EDTA molecules were covalently grafted onto the surface of SiO2 NPs by reaction between the anhydride group of EDTMD and the amino group of APTES. Eventually, Eu3+ coordinated with the EDTA ligands to obtain the INT-probe.

For material analyses, a series of characterizations were carried out. In Fig. 1B–D, the morphology of NPs was characterized by TEM. The images show that SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs are highly monodispersed with an averaged dimension of 647 ± 12 nm, and the further functionalization with EDTA and Eu3+ causes little change in size (654 ± 13 nm and 650 ± 12 nm for SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs and INT-probe NPs, respectively). In Fig. S4, DLS measurements were conducted to determine the hydrodynamic sizes of above NPs, and the diameters of 685, 671 and 678 nm were obtained with their respective polydispersity indexes of 0.104, 0.014 and 0.023, indicating good dispersity of the synthesized NPs.

In Fig. S5, the isoelectric points (IEP) of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs and SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs are estimated to be pH ∼8.1 and pH ∼4.7 according to the zeta potential profiles under different pH values. The decrease of IEP value indicates that EDTA is covalently modified on the surface of amino-functionalized SiO2 NPs. In Fig. S6, zeta potentials of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs, SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs and the INT-probe are 2.3 mV, −54.4 mV and −38.5 mV, respectively, suggesting the successive modification of EDTA and chelation of europium ions onto the surface of SiO2 NPs. Also, the effect of Eu3+ amount on the stability of INT-probe suspension was investigated by zeta potential. It is well-known that the larger the zeta potential absolute value is, more stable the suspension can be. As shown in Fig. S7, with the increase of the volume of added EuCl3 solution, the zeta potential of the resulting NPs changes toward positive-going direction, and their absolute values gradually decrease. To obtain the more stable probe suspension and higher detection sensitivity, the volume of EuCl3 solution was chosen as 10 μL to fabricate the INT-probe. In addition, the gradual addition of DPA to the INT-probe suspension causes changes of probes’ zeta potential toward negative-going direction and increases the absolute values of resulting NPs, suggesting a superior stability of INT-probe/DPA complex (Fig. S8).

In Fig. S9, the FT-IR spectrum of SiCDs shows the peak at approximately 1654 cm−1 attributing to C=ONR vibration, which confirms the acylation reaction between the carboxyl groups of CA and the amine groups of AEAPMS, suggesting the successful synthesis of SiCDs (Wang et al., 2011). Compared to FT-IR spectrum of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs, that of SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs exhibits a new peak at 1701 cm−1 and an enhanced peak at 1648 cm−1, which are attributed to carboxyl groups and amide groups (Jiang et al., 2019; Mikhraliieva et al., 2020), respectively, indicating the acylation reaction between the amine group of APTES and anhydride group of EDTMD and the modification of EDTA onto the surface of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs.

To further confirm the modification of EDTA onto the surface of SiO2 NPs, the high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s and N 1s for SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs and SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs are compared. In Fig. S10A, the C 1s band of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 287.7 eV, which are attributed to C–C, C–N and C–O, respectively (Mikhraliieva et al., 2020). Also, the N 1s band of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs is deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.2 and 400.9 eV (Fig. S10B), which are attributed to primary amine of APTES and secondary amine from SiCDs, respectively (Ravi et al., 2018). Different from the spectra of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs, the C 1s and N 1s bands in the spectra of SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs present new components at 288.8 eV (Fig. S10C) and 401.7eV (Fig. S10D) assigned to carbonyl and tertiary amine from EDTA, respectively (Ravi et al., 2018). These confirm the covalent modification of EDTA onto the surface of SiCDs@SiO2–NH2 NPs.

To analyze the composition of the INT-probe, XPS and elemental mapping analysis were performed. As shown in Fig. 1E, XPS spectrum demonstrates the presence of Eu 3d, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, Si 2s and Si 2p peaks centered at 1139.5, 532.2, 401.5, 286.6, 156.8 and 104.1 eV, respectively (Na et al., 2020). The high resolution XPS spectra of Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s indicates the presence of Si–O, Si–C, C–C, C–O, CO, C–N, O–H, –NH2, –NH and –N in the INT-probe (Fig. 1F-I). Fig. 1J exhibits peaks at approximately 1134.4 and 1164.6 eV, corresponding to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 components of Eu3+, respectively. Additional peaks at 1124.5 eV and 1154.4 eV are attributed to the shake-down satellites of Eu 3d5/2and Eu 3d3/2, respectively (Mercier et al., 2006). As illustrated in Fig. 1L–P, the elemental mapping analysis is indicative of the homogeneous distribution of Si, O, C, N and Eu elements throughout the INT-probe.

In this work, SiCDs are selected as internal reference because: (1) the absorption band of SiCDs extends to the deep UV region, which provides relatively strong emission under 254 nm UV LED; (2) SiCDs can condense together with TEOS when hydrolyzed so that they can be easily doped into SiO2 NPs (Dong et al., 2021), preventing SiCDs from leaking into solution; and (3) the fluorescence intensity of the SiCDs-doped SiO2 NPs can be easily adjusted depending on the volume of SiCDs colloid added into the initial reaction mixture for hydrolysis (Fig. S11) and the reproducibility of SiCDs-doped SiO2 NPs is excellent (Fig. S12). As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, SiCDs exhibit characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks at 240 nm and 360 nm. Two typical excitation peaks at approximately 240 nm and 360 nm under the emission of 460 nm are observed in fluorescence spectrum. Besides, SiCDs colloid features light yellow and bright blue under daylight and UV LED (254 nm), respectively.

Before detecting the real samples, the fluorescent response mechanism of the INT-probe for visual detection of DPA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2B, both SiCDs@SiO2@EDTA NPs (green line) and pure INT-probe (blue line) exhibit only one peak at 460 nm (from SiCDs) and they emit blue fluorescence. While no emission peaks are found for the single Eu3+ (black line). Owing to parity forbidden 4f-4f transitions, Eu3+ suffers from weak light absorption and low luminescence efficiency (Wang et al., 2021). However, Eu3+ can be sensitized by DPA (Binnemans, 2009). Because of the intense absorption bands of chromophore of DPA, more light can be absorbed by DPA than by Eu3+ itself. When DPA coordinates with Eu3+, the energy transfer takes place from the triplet state of DPA to one of the spectroscopic levels of Eu3+, so that Eu3+ can emit intrinsic fluorescence. With the addition of DPA (purple line, no fluorescence upon excitation), INT-probe/DPA complex (red line) exhibits characteristic emission bands (at 592, 620 and 700 nm) of Eu3+ ions (ascribed to 5D0→7FJ, J = 1, 2 and 4) upon irradiation at 270 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm (from internal reference SiCDs) of INT-probe/DPA complexes keeps almost unchanged (no obvious fluorescence response toward DPA), providing a reliable reference signal for colorimetric detection of DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of DPA via acoustofluidic platform
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the visual detection of DPA, an acoustofluidic platform was fabricated, containing all of the key components for fluorescence colorimetric assays, namely a BAWs-based device, a 254 nm UV LED equipped with a bandpass filter, a smartphone with a 200 × custom-designed magnification lens and a compact 3D printed chamber (whose inner walls were all painted black) to reduce light interference (Fig. S2). Scheme 2 shows the general procedure of DPA detection via acoustofluidic platform. First, the mixture suspension containing the analyte and the INT-probe is injected into the capillary from the inlet using a syringe. Second, when the suspension within the capillary remains stable, the BAWs-based device is actuated to concentrate the INT-probe NPs. Third, the fluorescence images of the enriched INT-probe aggregates in the capillary are taken by smartphone. To realize quantitative analysis of DPA, the obtained fluorescence images are transmitted to a computer via Wi-Fi, and the color information (RGB values) of the fluorescent aggregates is obtained with professional image analysis software (here, ImageJ).

Before detecting DPA, the detection conditions (pH, response time and response temperature) were optimized, and pH 7.4, 30 s and 25 °C were determined to provide high sensitivity in the following experiments (Fig. S13). Under a 254 nm UV LED, when BAWs are on, the obvious fluorescent aggregates centered along the capillary channel can be seen. With the increase of DPA, the color changes from blue to pink are observed using naked eyes (Fig. 3 A). However, when BAWs are off, it is hard to distinguish the fluorescence of randomly distributed NPs in the capillary (Fig. 3B), even if the INT-probe concentration is increased (Fig. S14). It is clear that the enrichment causes great enhancement of color signal and the sensitivity of visual detection can be improved. Movie S2 displays the dynamic process of acoustofluidics-based enrichment of DPA-sensitized INT-probes under excitation of UV LED. When acoustic is on, an obvious pink fluorescent aggregate can be formed within 2 min, demonstrating the rapidity of detection.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2022.114538.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3

For quantitative evaluation, the color information of fluorescent aggregates is obtained by ImageJ, and then calculated to R/B value. Briefly, the custom selection function (wand tool) is used to select the analysis area with a pixel color variation tolerance value of 30, then random points (here, 50) in the region of interest are picked to analyze their intensity values of the blue channel and red channel. The relative RGB value (R/B) is finally calculated and averaged to quantitatively evaluate the color change. As shown in Fig. 3C, R/B value versus the concentration of DPA ranging from 0.100 to 7.00 μM can be well fitted into a linear equation: R/B = 0.115cDPA + 0.439, R2 = 0.996. The lowest detectable concentration is 100 nM, which is far below the infectious dosage of the spores (60 μM required) (Zhang et al., 2008).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference and practicability of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a critical parameter in analytical methods. Hence, to evaluate the selectivity for detection of DPA, the colorimetric responses of the INT-probe toward potential interferences were investigated. The interferences include aromatic carboxylic acids with similar chemical structures like BA, o-PA, m-PA and p-PA, representative amino acids like Glu, Gly and Phe, common biologically related cations like Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+ and K+, anions like Cl−, NO3 − and HCO3 −, biomacromolecules like BHb and BSA, carbohydrate like glucose and blank matrix (diluted FBS). As shown in the Fig. 4 A (front bars), it could be seen that various substances lead to negligible changes in the R/B values and only DPA can induce an obvious fluorescence enhancement. This can be attributed to the fact that the carboxylate groups and the nitrogen atom in the pyridine ring of DPA can provide strong interactions with Eu3+ (Zhang et al., 2008) to form a tridentate structure with Eu3+ (Ai et al., 2009). The tridentate structure not only increases the rigid plane, but also shortens the distance between DPA and Eu3+, improving the energy transfer efficiency and enhancing the emission of the Eu3+. In contrast, other substances may not sufficiently chelate with Eu3+ and cannot enhance INT-probe fluorescence due to structural differences. When interfering substances coexist with DPA, the results in Fig. 4A (back bars) suggest that fluorescence response of the INT-probe toward DPA is scarcely influenced by other coexisting interfering substances with the same or higher concentration. These results confirm the high selectivity and anti-interference ability of the current INT-probe. Fig. 4B and C presents the corresponding photographs of fluorescent aggregates concentrated by BAWs-based device under excitation of a 254 nm UV LED.

Before the application of the developed method, the stability of the INT-probe and the reproducibility of visual DPA analysis were investigated. The INT-probes stored at 4 °C fridge was stable in 5 days (Table S1) and the fluorescence colors of the formed INT-probe/DPA aggregates were almost unchanged within 30 min (Table S2). Moreover, the reproducibility of the developed method is excellent. Detailed results are shown in Tables S3 and S4.

The effectiveness and reliability of the acoustofluidic platform were examined by analyzing DPA-spiked FBS samples. The serum samples were diluted 10 times before different levels of standard DPA solutions were added into the serum samples. DPA recoveries are between 97.7% and 110% and their relative standard deviations (RSD) are all less than 4% (Table S5), confirming the feasibility of the acoustofluidic platform in practical application. In addition, the developed method and other smartphone-based fluorescence colorimetric methods (Table S6) as well as similar acoustofluidic methods (Table S7) are compared. Table S6 shows that the proposed method is one of the most sensitive methods for visual detection of DPA, yet with the advantages of reduced lanthanide usage and simplified postprocess steps. Meanwhile, with the high reproducibility (RSD <5%) and short detection time (<5 min) (Table S7), the developed method is prospect for the future development of acoustofluidic-based POCT device.

In summary, a novel INT-probe has been successfully synthesized for the visual detection of DPA with high sensitivity and selectivity using acoustofluidic platform. The composition and properties of the integrated probe were characterized in detail by various analytical methods, including TEM, DLS, FT-IR, XPS, and element mapping. It has been demonstrated that the Eu3+-chelated sensing unit of the INT-probe can be trigged by DPA with low concentration but no response was received from internal reference, providing sensitively and reliable optical detection. By virtue of the home-made standing BAWs-based device, the INT-probe could be enriched and slight color change was amplified to emit intensely. So, the visual sensitivity could be greatly improved. With the increase of DPA, fluorescence changes of the INT-probe were observed from blue to pink, whose images were captured by smartphone and analyzed quantitatively through RGB values. To realize quantitative point-of-care detection in real samples, DPA-spiked fetal calf serum samples were analyzed, and the recoveries were between 97.7% and 110% (RSD <4%).

The above results demonstrate that the smartphone-based acoustofluidic method has a high sensitivity, accuracy and reliability to meet the requirement of the applicability. Especially, using smartphone as powerful outputs for optical imaging not only provides rapid optical detection of various colors, but also greatly reduces the costs of the diagnostic platform and makes the device more simplistic (Vashist et al., 2014, 2015a). However, as optical detectors, smartphone still has some limitations due to the frequently changing specifications of smartphone, environmental lighting interference, inevitable white balance corrections of smartphone camera and the undefined sensitivity variations between different smartphones (Vashist et al., 2015b; McCracken and Yoon, 2016). Future work will seek to further improve the smartphone-based sensing platform for commercially viable products. In addition, with the development of highly integrated and functional acoustofluidic device, ultrasensitive analyte-targeted nanoprobes and smartphone application in cloud computation, communication and big data, the next generation of smartphone-based acoustofluidic platform will be designed as an automatic POCT platform based on the internet to afford in-situ analysis with high accuracy not only in bioanalysis but also in environmental and food safety monitoring.

Jiafeng Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Panpan Chen: Data curation, Software, Validation. Jie Chen: Software, Formal analysis. Xiangxue Ye: Data curation, Validation. Shurui Cao: Data curation, Validation. Chuqiang Sun: Data curation, Validation. Yang Jin: Resources, Software. Liying Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Shuhu Du: Supervision, Conceptualization, Review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775073).
